questions.question: What is a hypervisor?
options: Software that manages and allocates hardware resources to VMs
A container runtime that runs Docker containers
A virtual switch inside a VM
A physical server used for bare-metal workloads
questions.explanation: A hypervisor (also called VMM) is software that manages and allocates hardware resources to virtual machines.

questions.question: Which description matches a Type 1 hypervisor?
options: Runs directly on hardware (bare-metal); used in data centers (e.g., VMware ESXi, Hyper-V)
Runs as a program on a host OS (hosted); used on personal devices (e.g., VirtualBox)
Runs inside a container engine like Docker
Runs only on Cisco UCS hardware
questions.explanation: Type 1 hypervisors run directly on hardware — bare-metal or native — used in data centers (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V). Type 2 runs on a host OS.

questions.question: What is a container?
options: A software package with an app plus its binaries/libraries/dependencies that shares the host OS kernel
A full OS running on virtualized hardware allocated by a hypervisor
A virtual switch that connects VMs to physical NICs
A Cisco hardware server product line
questions.explanation: A container is a software package containing an app plus its binaries/libraries/dependencies; it shares the host OS kernel. Unlike a VM, it doesn't bundle a full OS.

questions.question: What does VRF stand for?
options: Virtual Routing and Forwarding
Virtual Remote Forwarding
Virtual Resource Framework
Virtualized Router Function
questions.explanation: VRF stands for Virtual Routing and Forwarding — it divides one physical router into multiple virtual routers, each with its own routing table.

questions.question: Which cloud service model provides compute, storage, and network, with the customer managing the OS and apps (e.g., AWS EC2)?
options: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)
PaaS (Platform as a Service)
SaaS (Software as a Service)
FaaS (Function as a Service)
questions.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — provider supplies compute/storage/network; customer controls OS and apps (e.g., AWS EC2).

questions.question: Which is an example of SaaS?
options: Office 365 or Gmail
AWS EC2
AWS Lambda
VMware ESXi
questions.explanation: SaaS (Software as a Service) — provider supplies a finished application, such as Office 365 or Gmail.

questions.question: Which cloud characteristic means customers provision cloud resources themselves through a portal?
options: On-demand self-service
Broad network access
Resource pooling
Measured service
questions.explanation: On-demand self-service means customers provision cloud resources themselves through a portal — one of NIST SP 800-145's essential characteristics of cloud computing.

questions.question: Which command creates a VRF on a Cisco router in global config?
options: ip vrf <name>
ip vrf forwarding <name>
show ip vrf
vrf definition <name> exit
questions.explanation: `ip vrf <name>` creates a VRF in global config. `ip vrf forwarding <name>` assigns an interface to a VRF (and removes any configured IP).

questions.question: What is a Type 2 hypervisor?
options: Hypervisor that runs as a program on a host OS (hosted); used on personal devices (e.g., VirtualBox)
Hypervisor that runs directly on bare-metal hardware
Hypervisor that only runs containers
Hypervisor that runs inside a Type 1 hypervisor
questions.explanation: A Type 2 hypervisor runs as a program on a host OS (hosted); it's used on personal devices (e.g., VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox).

questions.question: What is a virtual switch (vSwitch)?
options: A software switch on a hypervisor that connects VMs and maps to physical NICs
A physical switch with virtual MAC tables
An SDN controller for physical switches
A container-level network bridge
questions.explanation: A virtual switch (vSwitch) is a software switch on a hypervisor that connects VMs and maps to physical NICs; it supports access/trunk ports and VLANs.

questions.question: Which cloud deployment model is infrastructure dedicated to a single organization?
options: Private cloud
Public cloud
Community cloud
Hybrid cloud
questions.explanation: Private cloud is infrastructure dedicated to a single organization. Public is open for general public use (AWS, Azure, GCP, OCI); community is shared by a specific group; hybrid is a combination.

questions.question: What does CapEx vs OpEx refer to in cloud discussions?
options: Capital expenses (upfront hardware) vs operating expenses (ongoing subscription); cloud typically shifts CapEx to OpEx
Capacity expansion vs operational expansion of the data center
Capital employment vs operational employment policies
Cloud expenditures vs on-premises expenditures only
questions.explanation: CapEx = capital expenses (upfront hardware); OpEx = operating expenses (ongoing subscription). Cloud typically shifts CapEx to OpEx.

questions.question: What is VRF-lite?
options: VRF used without MPLS; the form covered by the CCNA
A VRF that leaks routes between VRFs automatically
A lightweight hypervisor VRF instance
A VRF used only for IPv6 routing
questions.explanation: VRF-lite is VRF used without MPLS; it is the form covered by the CCNA.

questions.question: What is microservices architecture?
options: An architectural style that splits an application into many small services, often run in containers
A way to combine multiple monolithic apps into one VM
A type of hypervisor used for small workloads
A cloud billing model based on microservice usage
questions.explanation: Microservices is an architectural style that splits an application into many small services, often run in containers.
